text: The video shows a primary epigastric supraumbilical hernia located just above the umbilicus, with a large wad of incarcerated omentum visible.
The surgeon begins creating the peritoneal flap several centimeters lateral to the median fat pad. The peritoneum appears very thin, requiring careful dissection.

The surgeon uses their left hand to hold the peritoneal flap while carefully dissecting across to the contralateral side, avoiding grabbing the flap to prevent creating holes. The dissection progresses smoothly once reaching the median fat pad, allowing for blunt separation of tissues.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection to carefully separate the peritoneum from the posterior sheath, taking down adventitial fibers between these structures.

The surgeon uses their left hand to push down on the median fat pad while carefully dissecting with the right hand, avoiding grabbing the peritoneum. The flap is opened along the length of the incision for maximum exposure, and adjustments are made to the pocket size by extending it inferiorly. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the tissue layers without tearing the flap edges.
The surgeon dissects above and below the hernia defect, creating a 'volcano sign' for efficient dissection. The area around the umbilicus is visible, with a small umbilical hernia being reduced.
The surgeon completes the dissection by stopping at the loss of median fat on the contralateral side. The hernia defect is then reduced while carefully maintaining the integrity of the peritoneum.

The surgeon reaches the contralateral side, where the peritoneum appears thin and adheres closely to the posterior sheath. Using scissors, the surgeon performs blunt dissection, sweeping to create space and avoid perforating the peritoneum. As the dissection progresses laterally, the tissue separates similarly to the transversus abdominis release.

The surgeon gently pushes down to expose the adventitial tissue between the posterior sheath and the peritoneum. A pocket is created large enough to accommodate a fifteen by fifteen mesh, suitable for the size of the hernia defect and the patient.

The surgeon uses a Marlex mesh and dissects further laterally on the contralateral side to create redundancy in the flap. The dissection progresses easily as the peritoneum becomes very thin. The surgeon may enter the transversalis fascia if needed, similar to a TAR procedure, to ensure the flap can be sutured closed without tension.

The surgeon places a fifteen by fifteen mesh into the abdomen and begins suturing the defect using a zero V lock suture in a transverse orientation. The umbilical pseudosec is imbricated to create an 'innie' appearance and prevent seroma formation. The insufflation pressure is reduced to eight for the closure process.

The surgeon places a 3-0 silk suture at the center of the mesh before inserting it into the abdomen, aligning it through the center of the hernia defect. The mesh is positioned slightly to the right to ensure it is centered within the flap, providing a minimum of five to six centimeters of coverage around the hernia defect, likely closer to seven centimeters. The surgeon then secures the mesh by suturing the cardinal corners.

The surgeon manipulates the mesh on the ipsilateral side, ensuring it is positioned against the interior abdominal wall. The mesh is pulled up to prevent folding, potentially using a suture through the mesh to secure the corner.
